# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What movie made you cry the most?

**Score:** 103
**Body:** Hello, Idk if anyone can relate but once in a while I always just want to watch a (sad) movie to cry to. I already watched a lot of sad movies but most of them don't really make me cry so I was wondering if you can share the movies that really made you cry. And BONUS points if they made you cry out of happiness cause that feels amazing!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hachi: A Dog's Tale.   
  
Never ever again.
2. Dear Zachary will make u cry and break ur heart.
3. I am Sam, broke my heart
4. Aftersun broke me 😭. I was sobbing constantly for the last 20 minutes
5. A man called Otto. I was dead
6. Coco
7. My Girl. "HE CAN'T SEE WITHOUT HIS GLASSES" 😭
8. Room (2015) with Brie Larson

Had to pause it multiple times just so I could stop crying.
9. I watched Marley and Me a couple weeks after the dog I had for my entire childhood died.
10. Big Fish, Where the Red Fern Grows, Seven Pounds, the list is endless...

## 2. Mind benders

**Score:** 39
**Body:** Mind benders are my favorite genre of movie. Any recommendations? I've seen all the usual ones.

2001: A Space Odyssey (1968)

A Clockwork Orange (1971)

Eraserhead (1977)

Memento (2000)

Mulholland Drive (2001)

Donnie Darko (2001)

Black Swan (2010)

The Matrix (1999)

Inception (2010)

Primer (2004)

Coherence (2013)

Arrival (2016)

Annihilation (2018)

Everything Everywhere All at Once (2022)

The Truman Show (1998)

Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind (2004)

Synecdoche, New York (2008)

The Fountain (2006)

Under the Skin (2013)

Fight Club (1999)

Shutter Island (2010)

The Machinist (2004)

Oldboy (2003)


### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Perfect Blue

Paprika

Primal Fear
  - Perfect Blue is playing at an indie theater in my town all week. I am finally going to see it.
2. Brazil
3. Vivarium
4. Predestination, Arrival
5. Videodrome (1983)

Naked Lunch (1991)

Singapore Sling (1990)

Mandy (2018)
6. The Endless (2017)
7. Jacobs ladder 

The Prestige
8. Add 12 monkeys to ur list.
9. Time Crimes

## 3. I’m looking for some folk horror

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Lately into it but struggling to find good films, of course I’ve seen Midsommar, but also The Blair Witch Project, Wendigo, The Ritual, Ravenous, but I need more, any suggestion?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The OG Wickerman
  - Should be first on OP's list!
2. Viy
3. The witch is one of my favourites.
  - Forgot to mentioned it, but ofc I’ve seen it, terrific movie
4. 'Alucarda' (1977) crosses satanic horror with a bit of Mexican folk horror.
  - Yeahhh, marvelous movie, love ig
5. 'Hagazussa' (2017)

'Eyes of Fire' (1983)

'Witchfinder General' (1968)

'Black Sunday' (1960)
6. Apostle

Butterfly Kisses

A Field In England
7. Blood on Satan's Claw and Starve Acre. There is a documentary on it called Woodlands Dark and Days Bewitched: A History of Folk Horror that briefly covers a lot of films

## 4. Movies that have an (almost comically) angry police boss/chief/sergeant.

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Looking for suggestions where that character is pissed off at almost everything and everything for no good reason, can't dial it down, and has some great entertaining dialog.

Examples:

* Die Hard 2 - Dennis Franz as Carmine Lorenzo (Chief of Police of Dulles International Airport)
* Friday the 13th Part VI: Jason Lives - David Kagen as Sheriff Mike Garris
* Beverly Hills Cop - Gil Hill as Inspector Douglas Todd

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Last Action Hero
2. 21 jump street
  - 22 Jump Street (he's even angrier)
3. ‘So I married an Axe Murderer’ riffs on this in an awesome way
  - Came here to say this.

“And another thing captain. You’re too nice.”
4. Police Academy
5. Beverly Hills Cop
6. It’s just a scene but the Malibu sheriff (?) in The Big Lebowski could be what you’re looking for.

EDIT: grammar, spelling.
7. Smokey and the Bandit. 
8. 48 Hours

## 5. Looking for some solid thriller/mystery recommendations- both shows and movies. What are your favourites?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I'm in the mood for something suspenseful and gripping. Psychological thrillers, murder mysteries, or anything with a good twist would be great.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Series: 

The Outsider

Sharp Objects

True Detective 

The Killing 

Ozark


Movies:

Caddo Lake 

Presence

Watchers

Hold Your Breath

Speak No Evil

You Should Have Left

Watcher
2. The Illusionist is a great movie that I always see be overshadowed by the more popular The Prestige
3. Dial M for Murder (1954)

Rear Window (1954)

High and Low (1963)

Memento (2000)

Death Note (anime show)

Severance (TV show)
4. 8MM

Arlington Road

Wind River

Prisoners

Mystic River

Memories of Murder

The Chaser

Sleep Tight

The Vanishing (1988)

Series:

Extracurricular

Karma

Mask Girl

The King of Pigs
5. Unhinged, Man on Fire, Joyride(2001), Black Rain, Primal Fear
6. Mr Robot (show) - don't let the tech aspect dissuade you.  (Psychological-crime/drama/thriller)
7. I've watched Mystic River multile times. Sean Penn is an amazing actor, Laura Linney is spectacular and ruthlessly cold.

Prisoners plucks both your heart strings and the strings you want plucked for vengeance.  

The Vanishing is when I really started paying attention to Jeff Bridges.
8. Mr Brooks is about Dane Cook stalking a serial killer and he's surprisingly pretty good in it
9. Dead Again

Jack’s Back

F/X
10. The Last of Sheila. Both a good murder mystery and 1970s period piece.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name a prop that ruined a movie…

**Score:** 3873

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not a movie, but I will never stop laughing at the Nelly and Kelly Rowland music video for Dillema where she used excel to text her bf and got mad when he didn’t text back
  - This continues to be the greatest answer for all media
  - There is a scene in NCIS where 2 people type on the same keyboard at the same time, each "controlling" a different screen.

  
EDIT - [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u8qgehH3kEQ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u8qgehH3kEQ)
    - I love how the most popular YouTube clip of this was titled "2 idiots, 1 keyboard"
2. The hackey sack scene in twilight..  😁
  - Let's be honest.. many scenes in the  Twilight Franchise. That creepy baby...
3. The Cane from Citizen Kane.
4. I believe there's a scene in lord of the rings where they ride away on horses , and someone scoops up the hobbits , but they're clearly dummies
  - [wtf is up with pippin](https://i.redd.it/x91viw6kbpy41.jpg)
5. The mask they used for Halloween 4. It looks so cheap and flat, Michael Myers ends up looking funny more than terrifying

## 2. What movie would you say is 5 stars - basically perfect?

**Score:** 1065

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Terminator 2.
  - I saw terminator 2 a dozen times before I new there was a terminator 1 being born in the early 90s
2. The Lord of the Rings
3. Back to the Future
4. Gladiator
5. The Matrix
6. Raiders of the Lost Ark
7. The thing (carpenter one)
8. Die Hard
9. The Princess Bride

## 3. What is an amazing line that became common vernacular and almost no one knows where it came from?

**Score:** 926
**Body:** For three years I had roses and

I APOLOGIZED TO NO ONE.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Be afraid. Be very afraid," is probably more famous for all the times it was used for comedic purposes than its origin in The Fly, said by Geena Davis.
  - It's the tagline in the trailers / posters for the film too, right? So by all rights it should be famous like "In Space no one can hear you scream."
2. Mi Scusi (Eurotrip).
  - Worst. Twins. Ever!
3. One of us! One of us!
4. ” I do not think that means what you think it means”….Princess Bride
  - INCONCEIVABLE
5. So you’re telling me there’s a chance
6. Houston we have a problem. It's a misquote of the actual dialogue and much more famous.
  - Also "failure is not an option" is attributed to Gene Kranz, but he never said it. Ed Harris said it in the movie and it became so attached to the character, that Gene used it as the title of his own biography.

## 4. What are some of the greatest acting performances to not win an Oscar? Why?

**Score:** 480
**Body:** Jake Gyllenhaal’s nightmarish, psychopathic performance as the cold-blooded killer in Nightcrawler was considered to be not just the best performance of the year but as one of the finest of the decade.


There was no room for error in this movie, Jake didn’t want to play the character, he wanted to become the character and by doing so sacrificed much of his own life.

In the scene shown in the photo, Jake went so far that he shattered the mirror and sliced his thumb requiring 46 stitches in a 4 hour long operation - only to return back onto the set 6 hours later and complete filming.

However his thumb wasn’t the only thing he nearly lost, he lost weight as well - and a lot of it.

He starved himself and lost 30 pounds in order to become the embodiment of a Coyote
He did not blink for nearly the entirety of the movie
He dove himself into dark places to capture the essence of the role
But extreme methods aren’t always an attest to a good performance, I mean look at Jared Leto as Joker for an example - but for Jake, whatever commitment he brings to the role will always be reflected in his performance.

And there is no better example of this then in Nightcrawler


Here he plays Louis Bloom, a loner who discovers his strengths in recording footage of accidents and selling it to news channels for money.

Jake manages to capture this character to a frightening capability. As we see Louis lie, manipulate and eventually - kill, it’s all chillingly acted by Jake with his wide open hunter eyes.

And not only this but we in a perversive way root for Louis, he’s charming and he’s ambitious as well as being a psychopath and none of the above would be true without the careful balance and range of emotions we see.

It was carefully but brutally acted and is widely considered to be his best performance in a filmography that includes Brokeback Mountain, Southpaw and Prisoners.

But above all, he was disturbing and he was scary. Not many lead actors can ever pull that off.

So, how did the Academy snub Gyllenhaal?

Did they snub him out of Best Actor?

Nope,

They Snubbed him out of a nomination.

He wasn’t even recognised as one of the top 5 performances of the year.

That’s one surefire way to lose your credibility, Academy!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. William H. Macy as Jerry Lundegaard in *Fargo*
  - You betcha
2. OP asked AI to write about Nightcrawler and made a copy-paste mistake
3. Toni Collette in... Hereditary, The Sixth Sense etc.
  - Horror movies hardly get any recognition during Oscars.. Not even a passerby.
4. Edward Norton as Derek Vinyard in *American History X*.  He's believable to the point where it seems like the actor himself believes in what he's saying.
5. Val Kilmer in Tombstone
6. James McAvoy in Split/Glass. Amazing acting, mediocre movie.
7. This is such a safe answer but DDL in Gangs of New York
8. Ellen Burstyn in requiem for a dream. Gut wrenching performance, and unfortunately lost out because the academy were desperate to get Julia Robert’s an Oscar

## 5. Cillian Murphy saying he wasnt called for big studio roles after oppneheimer oscar win got to be the most disappointing thing EVER

**Score:** 400
**Body:** imagine winning an oscar and not getting sprawled with movie pitches and then we have pedro pascal sprinkled upon like salt in every god damn franchise with this fatigue now

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Him, Michael Shannon, Ben Mendelsohn & Tom Hardy deserve all the roles
  - Maybe a Twins remake with Cillian and Michael Shannon?
2. mans still getting work, and tbh it doesn’t bother he’s not getting leading roles like dicaprio, bale or phoenix, i think him not being big as them is what makes him one of my favorite actors
  - same man, he proved stardom doesnt really require a heavy recognition but i think he deserves a lot more man, like imagine gettling Netflix movies after a Nolan oscar hit
3. I don't think he should play the Hollywood game. He certainly doesn't need it.
4. We are overdue for a Peaky Blinders movie
  - It’s all done and will be out before the end of the year.
5. I'm still pissed he didn't get The Smashing Machine role.
6. An Oscar win isn't what it used to be. Look at Brendan Fraser he won an Oscar car and has only been in a small role in 2 movies.
  - Also happened to Olivia Colman. She didn’t really blow up after her win either.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Sinners: We missed the real meaning of the phrase: "These words were forced on us"

**Score:** 18
**Body:** In the end Remmick says these words as Sammie says the Lord's Prayer, and as such we assumed he was speaking about Christianity, but in history the Irish were Christian before the English invaded. The use of "words" specifically makes me think he's not talking about the religion, but English, the language, and the Latin alphabet. Irish had it's own indigenous alphabet. The prayer still gives him comfort because he was still Christian, it was the language, the alphabet that was forced on him, and Ireland. The erasure of culture, the erasure of community

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If he was just talking about English he could have mentioned that at any point in the film.


Christianity was still an external faith forced upon many Irish communities even if its entry doesn't coincide with English colonisation. Possibly, Remmick was from a community that was converted later in the process, its not like conversion and colonisation is a one and done process.
  - Additionally, we have no idea how old Remmick is. He is potentially over a thousand years old. It’s entirely possible he was alive when Christianity first reached Ireland. Focusing on the English language is to go out of your way to misread the film and its articulations about religion.
  - Except Christianity was not forced on the Irish, Patrick was a peaceful missionary and after him it was Irish people converting other Irish people. The christianization of Ireland is generally considered to be one of the most peaceful.

## 2. Who is the Enemy? Harold and Maude vs Dirty Harry

**Score:** 8
**Body:** spoilers covered in this essay and I like both of these movies and Harold and Maude is only 90 minutes.

If you live in America right now, you are very used to the outrage economy. Ever since the internet was accessible to normal people, but especially since Zoe Quinn's visual novel Depression Quest got positive reviews and got backlash from people who couldn't possibly understand why a game like that got positive reviews, there are billion-dollar industries based off of telling people who to be mad at and who to point the finger at. This has led to a country full of people who are mad as hell at society for completely opposing reasons. And this is far from the only time this has happened in American society. 

Many people say American cinema was at its highest when America was most divided as a country. The era of New Hollywood coincides with the assassinations of MLK, RFK, and Fred Hampton, The Weather Underground, The Kent State massacre, The Manson Murders, raging wars in Vietnam, the Black Power movement, the Sexual Revolution and Queer Rights. This moment of paranoia and distrust probably peaked during the Watergate crisis which to this date led to the only resignation of a US president. In this moment, Harold and Maude and Dirty Harry were made.

The themes of this era are attacked directly in both films, but they seem so opposed to each other in every aspect besides coming apart 3 days apart from one another. One is a tender existentialist romance, the other is a thrilling manhunt for an evil serial killer. The most important moment in Harold and Maude to me is a freeze frame of a holocaust tattoo one might barely notice while the most important moments in Dirty Harry are two powerful invocations of masculinity that are pop culture staples. Harold and Maude uses the at the time recent idea of having a contemporary songwriter in Cat Stevens soundtrack the movie. He gives the movie a kind of twee whimsical feel, reminding me of musical acts like Beat Happening where overt happiness could be subversive. Meanwhile Dirty Harry has someone who would become a Hollywood staple in Lalo Schifrin soundtrack the movie full of explosive, tense jazz. One sees suicide as something very serious, whether through Harold faking it to try to earn a display of concern from his distant mother or Maude doing it as a final period to her life, while Dirty Harry sees it as a nuisance done by the mentally ill who want attention. Behind his games Harold is a deeply introspective young man looking to be heard and accepted, whereas middle-aged Harry Callahan hates everyone equally and seeks to enforce order. Dirty Harry went onto be a successful franchise, and its titular character went on to be an icon of American masculinity, while Harold and Maude bombed and became a cult classic only people "in the know" would appreciate.
 
But they most differ in who they see as an enemy. The main villain of Harold and Maude, Harold's mother, may be more ignorant than malicious, but she nonetheless represents a type of conformity that suppresses emotion and sees every action as one done to preserve an image. It's not Harold's mental state she cares about, it's the image of having a mentally ill son who hasn't launched that impugns her so much. Her solution is to foist him onto some woman because marriage will make him look good or send him off to a war he is certainly not prepared to fight (seriously Harold looks like a twig). It's Maude who has seen where this type of conformity leads to and is thusly the one who grants Harold a new lease on life. 

By contrast the villain in Dirty Harry is an outsider to society, as shown by the peace sign and long hair. He uses these, and his good looks as a way to infiltrate society and harm vulnerable people. He is enabled by a legal system too hung up in appeasing an out of touch liberal elite to remember their main purpose is protecting the innocent and killing bad people. It thus makes sense it takes a vigilante cop like Dirty Harry to neutralize him before he kills anyone else.

I love both these movies, and it feels serendipitous that they came out so close to one another. Are there any other examples of equally opposing movies with similar proximity to each other?

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. The Conjuring: The Last rites - The demon as a filmmaker

**Score:** 0
**Body:** *Parental Anxiety, a stillborn schizophrenic child trying to find her own place in the world through love, intergenerational conflict (Father paranoid about his future son-in-law), the antique mirror as a fracture in the material world through which the immaterial (the demon) can assert its own will. A pseudo-historical text that slowly succumbs to fiction. Patrick Wilson's amazing performance as a father trying to battle his sickness while keeping tabs on everyone, a father too afraid to let his daughter go.*

*As you can see, too many thematic strands, too many signifiers screaming for signification. Too many characters, too safe, too fantastical to be scary. This aesthetic is a deceit in itself, which, in the end, looks like a family drama – which is its biggest strength, but not before becoming just a witness to an artifice – a collection of scare tactics.*

I know this reading might be obvious, dog-eared but listen. The demon is a filmmaker, trying to craft it's own space, adjusting the lighting, but all it knows are horror film tropes - doors creaking open, toys moving on their own, a dark space in the corner, an axe murderer, when there's a jumpscare coming, the lighting becomes dark, the space is isolated - it can play around with the subject - knock it around, make it float, make it scream. It represents the artform in it's infancy, how to make the immaterial presence known in the material world? Through a collection of cliche scary tricks, an artifice.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eh, I think you're reading too much into the text.  The movie never states that Judy is schizophrenic.  She's tormented by actual demons!  The movie is very clear that it believes that ghosts and demons are real, which has been the case throughout the Conjuring Universe.

"Intergenerational conflict" is a very hyperbolic way to describe Ed's behavior.  Ed doesn't want his daughter to get hitched to a guy she's been with for less than a year.  Very normal reaction for a father to have.

Lastly, everything about the Warrens is fictional.  The Smurls did report being tormented by spirits, but everything else is make-believe.

---
